exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar Work, Task 1: Present and Past

promptParts.before: We can go out now. It

questions.grammarRule: 'Now' signals an action happening at this moment — present continuous negative: 'is not raining'.
Past continuous describes a background action in progress at a specific past moment ('was waiting'), interrupted by a shorter action ('I arrived').

promptParts.before: Ann was waiting for me when I

questions.grammarRule: The shorter action that interrupts an ongoing background action takes simple past: 'arrived'.

promptParts.after: hungry. Let's go and have something to eat. (get)

questions.grammarRule: Present continuous can describe a changing/developing state happening now: 'I am getting hungry'.

promptParts.after: in your spare time? Have you got any hobbies? (you/do)

questions.grammarRule: Present simple asks about habitual/general activities: 'What do you do in your spare time?'.

promptParts.after: at the time of the accident? (the car/do)

questions.grammarRule: 'At the time of the accident' points to an action in progress at a specific past moment — past continuous: 'was the car doing'.

promptParts.after: me on Fridays but she did not phone last Friday. (phone)

questions.grammarRule: 'Usually' signals a habitual routine — present simple: 'Mary usually phones'.

promptParts.before: Mary usually phones me on Fridays but she

questions.grammarRule: 'Last Friday' is a specific past time marker — simple past negative: 'did not phone'.
Present continuous describes what's happening right now: 'What is happening?'.

promptParts.before: It's usually dry here at this time of the year. It

questions.grammarRule: A general habitual fact about the climate uses present simple: 'It doesn't rain much'.

promptParts.after: three times while we were having dinner. (ring)

questions.grammarRule: A completed action happening a specific number of times in the past ('three times') takes simple past: 'rang'.

promptParts.before: Yesterday evening the phone rang three times while we

questions.grammarRule: 'While' introduces an ongoing background action in the past — past continuous: 'were having dinner'.
The shorter action that interrupts a background situation takes simple past: 'went'.

promptParts.before: Linda was busy when we went to see her yesterday. She

questions.grammarRule: Past continuous describes what was already in progress at that past moment: 'was studying for an exam'.

promptParts.before: She was studying for an exam. We

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative, and 'want' (a stative verb) isn't normally used in continuous form: 'didn't want'.

promptParts.before: We didn't want to disturb her, so we

questions.grammarRule: This continues the simple past narrative describing a completed past sequence: 'didn't stay very long'.

promptParts.after: Tom the news, he didn't believe me. (tell)

questions.grammarRule: A single completed past action takes simple past: 'told'.

promptParts.before: When I first told Tom the news, he

questions.grammarRule: 'Believe' is a stative verb (describing a mental state) and isn't normally used in continuous form: 'didn't believe'.
'Thought that...' reports a specific past belief — simple past: 'thought'.
Past continuous can describe a temporary behavior happening at that time: 'was joking' (he thought I wasn't being serious).

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar Work, Task 2: Complete the questions using a suitable verb

questions.grammarRule: Present perfect connects a past action to its relevance now: 'Have you seen him?' (relevant to searching for him now).

promptParts.after: to bed so early last night?

questions.grammarRule: 'Last night' is a specific past time marker — simple past question: 'did you go'.

promptParts.after: ? Just to the post box. I want to post these letters. I'll be back in a few minutes.

questions.grammarRule: Present continuous describes a current or near-future planned action: 'are you going' (matches 'I'll be back in a few minutes').
Present simple asks about a habitual routine: 'Do you watch television every evening?'.

promptParts.before: Your house is very beautiful. How long

questions.grammarRule: Present perfect asks about a duration that continues up to now: 'How long have you lived here?'.
A specific completed past event (the holiday) takes simple past: 'Did you have a nice time?'.
'Recently' points to an unspecified time up to now — present perfect: 'Have you seen Julie recently?'.

promptParts.before: Can you describe the woman you saw? What

questions.grammarRule: Past continuous describes an ongoing state/appearance at a specific past moment: 'was she wearing'.

promptParts.before: I'm sorry to keep you waiting.

questions.grammarRule: Present perfect continuous asks about a duration continuing up to now: 'Have you been waiting long?'.

promptParts.after: to get to work in the morning?

questions.grammarRule: A general habitual fact uses present simple: 'How long does it take you...'.
'Yet' in a question about an expected action signals present perfect: 'Have you finished... yet?'.
'Ever' asking about life experience up to now signals present perfect: 'Have you ever been to the United States?'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar Work, Task 3: Choose the right alternative

promptParts.after: to work yesterday. She wasn't feeling well.

questions.grammarRule: 'Yesterday' is a specific finished past time — simple past: 'didn't go', not present perfect.
An action happening right now (as you look) takes present continuous: 'is wearing'.

promptParts.before: Your son is much taller than when I last saw him. He

questions.grammarRule: Present perfect describes a change with a visible result now: 'has grown a lot'.

promptParts.before: I still don't know what to do. I

questions.grammarRule: 'Yet' with present relevance signals present perfect: 'haven't decided yet'.

promptParts.after: so nice to me today. He isn't usually like that.

questions.grammarRule: 'Be' + adjective in the continuous form ('is being nice') describes temporary behavior, contrasted with 'is nice' for a permanent trait.

promptParts.before: Jane had a book open in front of her but she

questions.grammarRule: Past continuous describes an action not actually happening at that moment, despite appearances: 'wasn't reading it'.

promptParts.before: Marry wasn't happy in her new job at first but she

questions.grammarRule: Present continuous describes a change in progress right now: 'is beginning to enjoy it'.
'Have' (meaning 'possess' or 'there was') is a stative verb and isn't normally used in continuous form: 'didn't have'.

promptParts.after: it very difficult to get a job.

questions.grammarRule: Present perfect can describe an experience continuing to be true up to now, even without an explicit 'since' marker: 'has found it very difficult'.

promptParts.before: When Sue heard the news, she

questions.grammarRule: A specific past reaction at a specific past moment takes simple past: 'wasn't very pleased'.
Asking about permanent origin uses present simple, not present continuous: 'Where do you come from?'.

promptParts.before: I'd like to see Tina again. It's long time

questions.grammarRule: 'It's [a period of time] since + simple past' is the fixed pattern for describing time elapsed since a past event: 'since I saw her'.
